title: Clock Synchronization — Kerberos Clock Skew Related Authentication Failures

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.17 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.17 (Clock synchronisation) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.40: Clock Synchronization — Kerberos Clock Skew Related Authentication Failures.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.40' running on WinEventLog:Security, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4776 earliest=-24h
| where match(Status,"0xC000006A") OR match(Status,"0xC000052") OR match(_raw,"(?i)time|skew|clock")
| stats count by ComputerName, Status
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
description: Surfaces authentication failures that indicate clock skew risk for evidence integrity and Kerberos stability.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes windows and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: Surfaces authentication failures that indicate clock skew risk for evidence integrity and Kerberos stability — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Tune Status codes for your domain; (2) NTP remediation; (3) forensic timestamp trust. Indexes required: `index=windows`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Windows Security.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:Security`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Tune Status codes for your domain; (2) NTP remediation; (3) forensic timestamp trust.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4776 earliest=-24h
| where match(Status,"0xC000006A") OR match(Status,"0xC000052") OR match(_raw,"(?i)time|skew|clock")
| stats count by ComputerName, Status
```

Understanding this SPL

**Clock Synchronization — Kerberos Clock Skew Related Authentication Failures (A.8.17)** — Surfaces authentication failures that indicate clock skew risk for evidence integrity and Kerberos stability.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Windows Security. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: windows; **sourcetype**: WinEventLog:Security. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=windows, sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where match(Status,"0xC000006A") OR match(Status,"0xC000052") OR match(_raw,"(?i)time|skew|clock")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by ComputerName, Status** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Clock Synchronization — Kerberos Clock Skew Related Authentication Failures (A.8.17)** — Surfaces authentication failures that indicate clock skew risk for evidence integrity and Kerberos stability.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Windows Security. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart, Table
knownFalsePositives: Access reviews lag during HR transitions, manager changes, or quarterly review cycles.
